Interview · CarrierDigital Design · 2026

A complex business & systems problem

How I untangled pricing into a trusted, scalable system

Using a pricing redesign at Expedia Group as a vehicle to talk about systems thinking, tradeoffs, and cross-functional decision-making — not a UI tour.

Jeff Alford · Senior Experience Designer01
Context~1 min

Context

At Expedia Group, supply partners (hotels and vacation-rental hosts) manage reservations and payouts through a partner platform spanning web and native apps.

The reservation price breakdown sits at the center of that relationship — it’s how partners understand what a guest paid, what we kept, and what they get paid.

When partners can’t trust that number, they stop trusting the platform. So this surfaced as a UI complaint, but it was really a trust and business-model problem.

Jeff Alford · Senior Experience Designer02
The Problem~1 min

The Problem

Partners repeatedly contacted support because the existing price breakdown felt opaque and often didn’t match their internal systems or expectations.

Adding the Fenced MOD discount and the accelerator campaign doesn’t give the rate shown in the reservation. There is no trust in the rates we receive and how they are calculated.— Supply partner feedback


Business impact:

Eroded confidence in payouts and settlements, high support volume, and friction in a regulated financial ecosystem

Jeff Alford · Senior Experience Designer03
Constraints~2 min

Constraints

This is where the problem got genuinely hard — the design had to hold up across:

Business
5 business models (4 hotel + 1 vacation rental), each with different fees, commissions, taxes, and settlement logic.
Technical
One component system that renders on web and native, driven by inconsistent upstream pricing data.
Organizational
Finance, Tax, and Legal each owned rules I couldn’t override — correctness was non-negotiable.
Time & scale
Ship incrementally in a fast-paced roadmap while covering 20+ real-world edge cases.
Jeff Alford · Senior Experience Designer04
My Role~1 min

My Role

Lead Experience Designer, owning the end-to-end redesign across both platforms.

I owned
The information model, component system, interaction patterns, and the plain-language framework for explaining money.
I influenced
Which pricing rules surfaced to partners, and how Finance/Tax/Legal logic was translated into something human.
Partners owned
Product owned scope and sequencing; Engineering owned data accuracy and the rendering pipeline.
Jeff Alford · Senior Experience Designer05
Key Decisions~3 min

Key Decisions & Tradeoffs

We weighed three directions for the breakdown:

  • A — Mirror the ledger: fully accurate, but overwhelming and unreadable.
  • B — Layered disclosure: a clear summary with on-demand detail. The tradeoff: more engineering for tooltips/expansion, but it scales across all 5 models.
  • C — Simplify the numbers: cleanest UI, but it would hide regulated detail — a non-starter with Tax/Legal.

We chose B.

A configurable, reusable system — Title, Line item, Sub-line, Summary, tooltips — that separates guest payment from partner payout and answers “why this number?”.

Jeff Alford · Senior Experience Designer06
Outcome & Reflection~2 min

Outcome & Reflection

  • Shipped to production on web and native for both Hotels and Vacation Rental partners.
  • Improved partner understanding of payouts & settlements, reducing support contacts and confusion.
  • Retired a long-standing pain point — support had built their own calculator just to explain the old display.

If I did it again: I’d push earlier for a single shared pricing data contract, so design and engineering weren’t reconciling inconsistent inputs late in the process. I would have also built a more thorough catalog of all the possible scenarios and edge cases.

Jeff Alford · Senior Experience Designer07
How I WorkSignal

What this shows about how I work

Own complexity
I take ambiguous, high-stakes problems and give them structure.
Operate in ambiguity
No clean spec — I built the model that didn’t exist yet.
Speak both languages
Translate between executives, Finance/Legal, and engineering.
Think beyond screens
Designed a system and a vocabulary, not just layouts.
Raise the bar
Reusable, high-craft components others can build on.
Jeff Alford · Senior Experience Designer08
At CarrierBridge

How this maps to your work

The same approach travels to any domain where complex, regulated systems meet real users:

  • Find the real problem under the UI request
  • Make the system’s logic legible without dumbing it down
  • Build reusable components that scale across products and platforms
  • Partner deeply with engineering and domain experts to keep it correct

Happy to go deeper on any decision — and I have other examples (figmas, edge cases) to pull from in discussion.
